title: Organise a Community Forum
description: Plan and run a community forum on a local issue — venue, speakers, promotion, facilitation, and follow-up. This is grassroots democracy in action.
whyItMatters: Community forums are where local democracy happens. They connect residents with decision-makers, surface issues that don't make the news, and build the networks that campaigns and movements rely on. Every successful politician has organised or spoken at dozens of these.

steps.title: Choose the issue and format
steps.description: Select a live local issue (development, transport, healthcare, education). Decide the format: panel discussion, town hall Q&A, or workshop.
steps.tips: Keep the scope focused. 'Proposed development at Smith Street' is better than 'the future of our suburb'.

steps.title: Secure venue and speakers
steps.description: Book a venue (community hall, library, school). Invite 2-3 speakers with different perspectives — a local councillor, a community advocate, and an expert.
steps.tips: Invite the local MP too. If they decline, note their absence publicly.

steps.title: Promote the event
steps.description: Create flyers, social media posts, and a community email. Distribute through local networks, school newsletters, and community noticeboards.
steps.tips: Personal invitations get better turnout than flyers. Ask 10 people to each bring 5 friends.

steps.title: Facilitate the forum
steps.description: Open the forum, introduce speakers, manage Q&A, keep time, ensure diverse voices are heard. Close with clear next steps.
steps.tips: Set ground rules at the start: respect, time limits on questions, no personal attacks.

steps.title: Document and follow up
steps.description: Write a summary of what was discussed, decisions made, and actions committed. Send to all attendees and relevant decision-makers.
steps.tips: The follow-up is where the real value lies. A forum without follow-up is just venting.

deliverables: Event plan with timeline and budget
Attendance list with contact details (with consent)
tools: Eventbrite or Google Forms for RSVPs

rubric.description: Is the issue genuinely important to the local community?
Did the forum include speakers and attendees with different perspectives?
Was the forum well-managed, inclusive, and productive?
Were outcomes documented and communicated to attendees and decision-makers?
Did the forum contribute to real progress on the issue?
